issues_found: Ground truth definition is circular: 'true absorption rate' (Section 3.2) and 'collision rate' (Section 3.4) are both defined as Jaccard overlap of top-K features. The Results present their correlation as 'proxy validation,' but it is self-correlation of the same metric.

Only 7 ground truth absorption pairs for UAD evaluation (one is a self-pair [24189, 24189], so 6 distinct pairs). With 6-7 positives and 4155 detected candidates, statistical power is extremely limited. The F1=0.00048 figure has huge variance---a single additional true positive would change F1 by ~40%.

The paper claims 'absorption features are mutually exclusive at the token level' as a universal property, but this is only demonstrated for number words in a single constructed sequence. Real-world hierarchical concepts (e.g., 'animal' and 'dog') DO co-occur in natural text.

The abstract claims 'F1 = 0.0005, indistinguishable from random sampling (same-cluster random F1 = 0.00048).' These values are identical to 4 significant figures, but this is a mathematical identity, not a statistical finding. Both UAD and same-cluster random have exactly 1 TP, 4154 FP, 6 FN.

The K-means ablation achieves 85.7% recall (6/7 TP) but the paper dismisses it as 'still failing.' This is inconsistent---85.7% recall is actually quite good for an unsupervised method. The issue is precision (0.043%), but the paper does not discuss whether the precision problem is addressable.

The novelty check was performed on the original positive-result UAD proposal, not the current negative-result paper. The novelty of 'UAD doesn't work' is different from 'UAD is a new method.' The core insight is logically trivial once stated.

The paper tests UAD on only one SAE (gpt2-small-res-jb, layer 8) with 1,000 sequences. The claim that 'the root cause is architecture-independent' is unsupported. Different SAE architectures may learn different feature structures.

The ablation results show that 'no dead filter' and 'no phi filter' produce IDENTICAL results to full UAD (same TP/FP/FN counts). This is suspicious---removing filtering steps should change the candidate set. The identical counts suggest these filtering steps are no-ops.

The paper's tone oscillates between 'harsh rejection of co-occurrence' and 'constructive forward look.' In the Conclusion, it calls on the community to 'abandon co-occurrence-based approaches'---this is stronger than the evidence supports.

The F5 false positive analysis claims '99.98% false positive rate' but this is computed as FP/(TP+FP) = 4154/4155. This is the standard definition of 1 - precision, but the term 'false positive rate' in ML typically means FP/(FP+TN).
issues_fixed: Eliminated fabricated claims from Iter 2 (no hardcoded/fabricated results appear)
Pivoted honestly to negative-result framing (UAD failure is the core finding)
Expanded collision rate validation from n=10 (Iter 1) to n=56 with bootstrap CIs (r=0.87, CI=[0.78, 0.94])
Increased GT sample from 2 pairs (Iter 1-2) to 6-7 pairs (Iter 3)
Clean data-claim alignment: all numerical claims in paper match raw JSON data (verified by writing review)
Thorough ablations: all UAD variants tested, not just full pipeline
Honest limitations section with 4 explicit limitations mapped to future work
